title: Net official development assistance to medical research and basic health sectors
subtitle: Net [official development assistance](#dod:oda) to medical research and basic health sectors. This data is expressed in US dollars. It is adjusted for inflation but does not account for differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Net official development assistance to medical research and basic health sectors, 2023

Net official development assistance to medical research and basic health sectors. This data is expressed in US dollars. It is adjusted for inflation but does not account for differences in living costs between countries.

The choropleth map displays the geographic distribution of net official development assistance directed toward medical research and basic health sectors across the globe for the year 2023. The visualization utilizes a continuous color scale ranging from pale yellow, representing zero dollars, to deep navy blue, indicating values exceeding 300 million US dollars. Areas shaded in grey, including the United States, Canada, Australia, and most of Western Europe, denote regions where no data is reported, consistent with their status as donor rather than recipient nations in this dataset. The highest concentrations of financial assistance appear as dark blue patches clustered in specific regions of Africa and South Asia. Ethiopia, Sudan, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo stand out in the darkest band, signifying inflows greater than 300 million US dollars. India and Pakistan also occupy this upper tier of the scale, showing similarly high levels of recorded assistance. Moving down the scale, countries such as Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, and Nigeria fall within the medium-to-dark blue bands, corresponding to values between 100 million and 300 million US dollars. In South America, Brazil and Colombia appear in lighter teal shades, placing them in the 30 million to 100 million dollar range. Large portions of the map, particularly across North Africa, the Middle East, and Central Asia, are covered in pale yellow or light green, indicating assistance levels between zero and 30 million US dollars. The data shows a distinct concentration of high-value aid flows into East Africa and the Indian subcontinent, while the rest of the developing world receives comparatively lower amounts.
